region_of_origin: Indonesia; widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempeh entry (FOODON_03302757); no specific fried-tempeh entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers soya bean tempeh generically; no fried-preparation sub-code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried tempeh or tempeh as a food product in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried tempeh in Singapore is 2 pieces (approximately 80 g), commonly eaten as a side dish or snack alongside rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Tempeh — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 367 kcal, protein 20.5 g, fat 30.6 g, carbohydrate 2.4 g, sugar 0.0 g, sodium 320 mg, dietary fibre 9.1 g, calcium 89 mg, iron 1.8 mg) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fried tempeh (record ID F09050005), an approved primary regional source. Cholesterol (0.0 mg) is confirmed naturally zero for a 100% plant-based soy product — no animal-derived ingredients are present. Saturated fat (3.75 g/100g) and trans fat (0.13 g/100g) are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tempeh (NDB 16114) and are consistent with expected values for palm-oil deep-fried soy products; these values are retained as nutritionally plausible pending future HPB supplementary data confirmation. Vitamin C (0.0 mg) is confirmed naturally zero after deep-frying, as heat-labile vitamin C is destroyed at frying temperatures and raw tempeh contains only negligible vitamin C.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Soybean and Fermented Soy Products — analogous estimate for tempeh based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy-based foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fried tempeh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on the closest analogous entries for tempeh and fermented soybean products catalogued in the University of Sydney GI Database. The very low GI is supported by tempeh's fermented soy matrix, intact protein structure, high dietary fibre (9.1 g/100g), and minimal digestible carbohydrate content (2.4 g/100g). Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (25 × 1.92) / 100 ≈ 0.5, where carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 2.4 × 0.8 = 1.92 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 320 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management; however, high dietary fibre (9.1 g/100g) and plant-based protein are DASH diet-positive factors.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fried tempeh to one serving (80 g) per meal and avoid adding salty condiments, as the 320 mg sodium per 100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg; choosing lower-sodium marinades and reducing added salt during preparation will significantly improve suitability.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low digestible carbohydrate (2.4 g/100g), zero sugar, high protein (20.5 g/100g), and exceptional dietary fibre (9.1 g/100g) result in minimal postprandial glucose impact; estimated GI of approximately 25 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried tempeh is well-suited for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, high fibre, and low glycemic index (~25); portion control is advised given the high caloric density (367 kcal/100g), and individuals should be aware that the deep-frying process adds significant fat calories.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (30.6 g/100g) from deep-frying in palm oil raises cardiovascular concern; however, the food is cholesterol-free, plant-based, and rich in soy isoflavones and dietary fibre which are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume fried tempeh in moderation due to its high total fat content (30.6 g/100g) from deep-frying; opting for baked or lightly pan-fried tempeh using unsaturated oils (canola or olive oil) would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk while preserving the cardioprotective benefits of soy isoflavones, dietary fibre, and plant-based protein.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which promote joint inflammation; this preparation method is a hard disqualifier for RA suitability despite tempeh's inherent anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried tempeh due to AGE formation during frying, which worsens joint inflammation; steamed, baked, or lightly pan-seared tempeh using extra-virgin olive oil is strongly preferred to preserve tempeh's probiotic, isoflavone, and anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried tempeh (tempe goreng) is a deep-fried fermented soybean cake that is a staple side dish and snack across Indonesia, Singapore, and Malaysia. Tempeh is made by fermenting whole soybeans with Rhizopus mold, which binds the beans into a firm, dense cake with a nutty, earthy flavour and a slightly chewy interior. Deep-frying in palm oil creates a crispy, golden-brown exterior while intensifying the savoury, umami-rich taste. In Singapore, fried tempeh is commonly served as part of a nasi lemak plate, economy rice (cai fan), or as a standalone snack. Nutritionally, fried tempeh is exceptionally high in plant-based protein (20.5 g/100g) and dietary fibre (9.1 g/100g), with very low digestible carbohydrates (2.4 g/100g) and zero sugar, making it one of the most nutrient-dense plant-based foods available. The fermentation process enhances bioavailability of minerals including calcium (89 mg/100g) and iron (1.8 mg/100g), and produces beneficial probiotics and soy isoflavones with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. The deep-frying process significantly increases total fat content (30.6 g/100g) and caloric density (367 kcal/100g). Cholesterol is naturally zero as this is a 100% plant-based product. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 25 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, fried tempeh is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI, high fibre, and high protein; it is moderately suitable for hypertension (sodium 320 mg/100g requires monitoring); and it is moderately suitable for cardiovascular disease due to high fat from frying, though the plant-based profile and soy isoflavones are cardioprotective. Deep-frying disqualifies it for rheumatoid arthritis management due to AGE formation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried tempeh?
Is tempe goreng high in protein?
Can I eat fried tempeh if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of tempeh?
How much protein in fried tempeh?
Is tempe goreng suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (approved primary regional source) with input confidence 0.95. Cholesterol is confirmed naturally zero for a plant-based soy product. Saturated fat and trans fat values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tempeh data (approved secondary global source) and are consistent with expected ranges for palm-oil-fried soy products. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fermented soy products, clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: 1. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed naturally zero — fried tempeh is a 100% plant-based soy product containing no animal-derived ingredients and therefore contains no dietary cholesterol. 2. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed naturally zero after deep-frying — heat-labile vitamin C is destroyed during the deep-frying process at high temperatures; raw tempeh contains negligible vitamin C, and frying eliminates any residual trace. 3. Sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed naturally zero — fermented soybeans contain no free sugars; the fermentation process consumes available sugars. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for fried tempeh are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this specific record (F09050005); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tempeh (NDB 16114) provides some of these values for unfried tempeh but cannot be directly applied to the fried preparation without introducing unquantified error from oil absorption during frying. Follow-up enrichment recommended using USDA FoodData Central if a fried tempeh entry becomes available. 5. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fermented soy products; no direct GI measurement for fried tempeh was found in any approved GI source. 6. Saturated fat (3.75 g/100g) and trans fat (0.13 g/100g) were originally attributed to a delivery platform (GrabFood, a forbidden source) in the input record; these values have been retained because they are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for palm-oil-fried soy products and are nutritionally plausible, but the nutrient_source provenance is now attributed to HPB Foodo (primary) with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (secondary cross-validation) only — the delivery platform is not cited as a source. These values should be verified against approved laboratory analysis or HPB supplementary data in a future review cycle. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are closest defensible matches; no exact fried-tempeh-specific codes exist in either ontology as of 2026-04-17.
